model_output_raw_text: {"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 8","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 64","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 32","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The optimizer and short-horizon schedule are already much stronger than baseline; the next clean lever is modestly increasing model width so the network can use those 256 steps more effectively without changing training logic.","secondary_modes":["topk","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),","function":"","new":"","old":"","op":"insert_after","source":"","text":"            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),"}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The widened network improved substantially, so the next low-risk capacity increase is one more same-resolution residual refinement block. This keeps the successful optimizer and schedule intact while adding a small amount of extra depth.","secondary_modes":["summaries","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Under a fixed 256-step budget, a larger batch exposes the model to substantially more distinct training examples. The optimizer and schedule are already stable, so increasing batch size is a clean way to trade some per-step cost for better short-horizon data coverage.","secondary_modes":["topk","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"After widening, deepening, and doubling batch size, the current setup is likely slightly under-driving AdamW during the short 256-step horizon. A modest learning-rate increase is the cleanest next move to convert that extra capacity and data coverage into faster loss reduction.","secondary_modes":["micro","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The move from batch 64 to 128 gave a large gain, which suggests short-horizon data coverage is still a dominant factor. Increasing to batch 256 is the next simple test to expose the model to nearly the full dataset within the fixed 256-step budget.","secondary_modes":["topk","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.05)","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.1)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"With batch 256 the model now sees nearly the full dataset within the fixed horizon, so the current 0.1 label smoothing may be slightly too strong and can blunt confidence. Reducing it to 0.05 is a small regularization adjustment aimed at improving validation loss without changing compute or optimization dynamics.","secondary_modes":["micro","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"summaries","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    warmup_steps = min(max(steps_per_epoch // 8, 1), max(total_steps // 20, 1))","old":"    warmup_steps = min(max(steps_per_epoch // 4, 1), max(total_steps // 10, 1))","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"summaries","rationale":"With batch 256 and a stronger 3e-3 AdamW setup, the current 25-step warmup is likely longer than needed inside a 256-step budget. Shortening warmup should let the run spend more of its limited horizon near an effective learning rate while keeping the same cosine decay shape.","secondary_modes":["topk","micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 2e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The run now trains on nearly a full-epoch equivalent with strong data coverage, but still only for 256 optimizer steps. At this horizon, the current 5e-4 AdamW decay may be a bit too restrictive; reducing it is a cheap way to let the model fit faster without adding compute.","secondary_modes":["topk","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 3.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 2e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The lower weight decay moved in the wrong direction, so restore the stronger AdamW regularization from the best run and test a small learning-rate increase on top of the shortened warmup. That is the cheapest next way to push more optimization progress out of the same 256-step budget.","secondary_modes":["caching","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 3.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 128","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The higher learning rate overshot, so return to the stronger 3e-3 setting and use the next low-cost capacity lever instead: a larger classifier head. That adds little runtime compared with widening convolutional blocks but can still improve class separation within the same 256-step budget.","secondary_modes":["topk","micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),\n            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),\n            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),","old":"            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The larger classifier head helped, so the next direct capacity test is one more same-resolution residual refinement block. This keeps the successful optimizer, batch size, and schedule unchanged while increasing feature processing depth in the backbone.","secondary_modes":["micro","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),","old":"            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"Adding the third residual block produced another meaningful loss drop, so the next most direct follow-up is one more same-width residual refinement block. This preserves the current successful optimization recipe and tests whether depth is still buying useful accuracy within the fixed-step budget.","secondary_modes":["micro","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),","old":"            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"Depth has been the strongest lever in the current regime, with each added residual block continuing to pay off. The next straightforward experiment is one more same-width residual block while keeping the now-strong optimizer, batch size, and schedule fixed.","secondary_modes":["micro","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),","old":"            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"Depth remains the strongest empirical lever by a wide margin, and the last added block still delivered a large gain. The next step is another same-width residual block, keeping every other successful setting fixed to test whether that depth trend continues.","secondary_modes":["micro","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 18","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),","old":"            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The sixth residual block overshot the current sweet spot, so step back to five blocks and spend the extra capacity budget on a small width increase instead. That tests whether the current regime now prefers slightly richer channels over more sequential refinement depth.","secondary_modes":["micro","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 18","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.05)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The width increase was clearly not worth its compute cost, so return to the stronger 16-channel backbone and use the remaining budget on a cheaper regularization test. With the model now much deeper and training on large batches, removing label smoothing may let it fit the task more sharply without any extra runtime.","secondary_modes":["layout","micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.05)","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The no-smoothing variant regressed, so restore the better regularization and test a slightly lower learning rate for the now-deep 5-block model. After several depth increases, 3e-3 may be a bit aggressive; a small reduction could improve final validation loss without changing compute.","secondary_modes":["caching","layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"summaries","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    warmup_steps = min(max(steps_per_epoch // 16, 1), max(total_steps // 40, 1))","old":"    warmup_steps = min(max(steps_per_epoch // 8, 1), max(total_steps // 20, 1))","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"summaries","rationale":"The best run so far came from lowering the learning rate while keeping the deeper 5-block model, which suggests the schedule is close but may still be spending too much of a 256-step budget in warmup. A final warmup reduction is a targeted way to spend more steps near the effective peak learning rate without changing model capacity or compute.","secondary_modes":["topk","layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"summaries","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    warmup_steps = min(max(steps_per_epoch // 8, 1), max(total_steps // 20, 1))","old":"    warmup_steps = min(max(steps_per_epoch // 16, 1), max(total_steps // 40, 1))","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"summaries","rationale":"The shortened warmup regressed, so the strongest final move is to restore the previous schedule that produced the best visible loss with the 5-block, 2.5e-3 configuration. That reverts to the best-known setting rather than spending the last step on a lower-confidence change.","secondary_modes":["topk","layout"]}
